text: So the first step in these surgical procedures is to open the capsular bag with OVD.
And using a cystotome to identify where you can go underneath the capsular edge is really the first critical step.
Once you've identified that spot, you go all around and inject viscoelastic to inflate the capsular bag.
If you have overinflated, you do want to burp the viscoelastic out so you don't have an overinflated capsular bag.
Like I said, injecting that OVD underneath the capsulorexus margin is the critical step, and sometimes that step can be quite challenging, especially if you have fibrosis of the anterior capsule.
I really find that this cannula, called the Flax cannula, it was originally designed for flax, but it's a very, very good cannula for IOL exchange.
It has a spatulated end, and you can see that it's actually a cannula, so you can attach it
to viscoelastic and the spatulated end really becomes useful as you try to separate the anterior capsule from the
capsular excess margin.
So if you see here, this spatulated end will easily go behind that capsular edge very easily.
And once you do that, it's kind of game over because you can inject viscoelastic and inflate the capsular bag.
As you do that, the anterior and posterior capsules will begin to separate, and then you can go all the way across and ensure that you have capsular separation all around.
This is another example where we have much more fibrosis, and you'll see the value of the flax cannula.
You can see here that the spatulated end easily goes underneath that fibrosed edge of the capsulorexis and then begins to inflate the capsular bag.
And once that inflation happens, the surgical procedure becomes much more simple.

In situations where there's a lot of fibrosis of the anterior capsule, the question is how do you get that optic out of the fibrose capsular bag, especially if that diameter is quite small compared to the optic of the intraocular lens.
And really the choices are you cut this optic
with the lens staying in the capsular bag, so that's certainly an option.
But in situations where you don't have too much fibrosis outside of the very edge of the capsulorexis, you certainly can indeed recut at the edge of the capsulorexis and tear all around.
And that's what we're doing here.
There's fibrosis at the edge, certainly, but no fibrosis beyond that.
So we're really doing a second capsulorexis in this case
so that we can enlarge the capsular opening and then begin to prolapse the intraocular lens out of the eye.
So here we've enlarged the capsulorexus and then we go about getting that intraocular lens into the anterior chamber.
When you have to get that lens out into the anterior chamber, which is the step you will do once you have an adequate opening, there are some nuances to keep in mind.
You really want to visualize the tip of the haptic when you're trying to dial that lens out of the bag.
You can use iris hooks to achieve that goal like you can see here.
And you really want to visualize the haptic completely when you're trying to dial out because the knob that you have in the Alcon lenses, for instance, really can have a lot of fibrosis around it.
And if you're not careful and you continue to dial, you'll have a zonular dialysis.
So inspecting that area as you're dialing the lens is really key.
In situations where the
haptic is stuck in the equator, the dialing will not get that haptic out easily.
And in such situations, using a two-handed technique where this Szynski hook keeps the IOL with tug on the region of the haptic, and using a second Szynski hook
can help free the knob at the edge of the haptic in some situations.
In other situations, the fibrosis is very dense, and in those situations you'll have to snip the haptic and leave that haptic in the capsular bag, and that's what we're doing here.
We were successful in releasing the haptic there.
So if it really won't release and it's very firmly stuck, you really have to cut it and leave the rest of the haptic in the capsular bag.
The next step is to cut the intraocular lens.
It's fairly straightforward if you use the right instrumentation.
The Chang Packard scissors are an excellent choice, and then you get the intraocular lens out through the clear corneal incision.
inflate the capsular bag with viscoelastic like we did in the previous cases, place the entire capsular bag in an inflated position, and then cut the area of fibrosis with a Grishaber scissor.
In this instance here, we have an area where we are trying to exchange the crystal lens.
The capsulorexus margin is really outside of the optic of the intraocular lens.
There's an area here with a lot of fibrosis.
And we are using the same FLAX cannula that I mentioned previously, where the
truncated edge of that flax cannula really can help lice areas that are fibrosed.
And you'll see that in a short while.
And we had actually created
a central capsulotomy for posterior capsular opacification in this case.
When you inject viscoelastic in such cases, because you have a posterior capsular break, you want to be very careful about how much viscoelastic you inject.
If you inject a lot of viscoelastic, it's going to go through this posterior capsular opening and further enlarge that posterior capsular break.
sparing in how much of inflation of the capsular bag you do.
We've done that here, and now we're going to go about cutting the intraocular lens that's in situ.
And thereafter, we get the intraocular lens out like we normally do.
We're checking here for any vitreous, and then we'll go about inserting a three-piece intraocular lens and capturing that optic of the intraocular lens in the intact capsulorexis that we had.
The other situation that I want to quickly talk about are situations where the entire capsular bag with the intraocular lens in it has subluxed.
And those are situations where you start by creating a Hoffman pocket.
So we are doing that perpendicularly to Hoffman pockets created with a crescent blade.
And once the Hoffman pockets have been created, there's vitreous here, so we're going to do a shallow anterior vitrectomy.
And then we're going to pass a 27-gauge needle.
The needle has to engage the capsular bag posterior to the haptic.
you then get one double arm suture anterior and one double arm suture posterior to the haptic so that the two suture ends of the double arm suture can then be tied in the Hoffman pocket.
So that's really
The principle that we're going to follow in such cases, here we've passed both the sutures and we're going to tie the knot in the Hoffman pocket and get the intraocular lens well centered.
We've talked about that.
It's a fairly straightforward step to do.
The Hoffman pocket needs to be about half skeletal thickness deep.
not deeper than that, and about 3 millimeters wide.
And you want to mark the edges of that Hoffman pocket.
One of those will go posterior to the haptic and the second one will go anterior to the haptic of the intraocular lens.
So essentially those are the steps that you do in these cases where the
intraocular lens is in the capsular bag, but the whole capsular bag is prolapsed.
When you're passing those sutures, you want to make sure that you're using some counter pressure.
If you don't, you can tear the zonules that are existing in such a case.
